expanded_description:
The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks) lend reserve balances to each other overnight on an uncollateralized basis. It is the most closely watched interest rate in the world because it is the primary policy target of the Federal Reserve. By setting a target for the federal funds rate and using its monetary policy tools to keep the actual rate within that target range, the Fed influences all other interest rates—from mortgage rates to corporate bond yields—and ultimately economic activity, employment, and inflation.

Understanding the federal funds rate is essential for analyzing how the Fed implements monetary policy, how changes in policy transmit to financial markets, and why the federal funds rate is the benchmark for short-term interest rates globally. The Fed does not directly control the federal funds rate; rather, it influences it through the supply of reserves and the interest rate it pays on reserves (IORB). The actual federal funds rate is determined by the supply and demand for reserves in the overnight interbank lending market.

In this section, we will examine the mechanics of the federal funds market, how the Fed sets and implements its target, the relationship between the federal funds rate and other interest rates (the transmission mechanism), the role of the federal funds rate in monetary policy rules (Taylor rule), and the history of federal funds targeting including the zero lower bound period. You will learn to interpret FOMC statements, understand the dot plot, analyze the spread between the federal funds rate and IORB, and evaluate the effectiveness of the federal funds rate as a policy tool in different economic environments.

core_concept.principle:
The federal funds rate is the overnight interest rate at which banks lend reserves to each other. It is the Federal Reserve's primary policy target.

Key Concepts:

What are federal funds? Reserve balances that depository institutions hold at their regional Federal Reserve Bank. Banks with excess reserves lend to banks with reserve deficiencies.

Why do banks borrow and lend reserves?
• To meet reserve requirements (though requirements are now 0%, banks still need reserves for clearing and payments)
• To manage daily liquidity fluctuations (deposit withdrawals, large wire transfers)
• To earn interest on excess reserves (though IORB changes the calculus)

Federal Funds Rate Determination:
• Pre-2008 (scarce reserves): Determined by supply and demand for reserves. The Fed used open market operations to shift supply to hit its target.
• Post-2008 (ample reserves): The Fed sets IORB (interest on reserves) as a floor under the federal funds rate. The actual fed funds rate trades between the ON RRP rate (floor for non-banks) and IORB (floor for banks).

FOMC Target:
The Federal Open Market Committee announces a target range for the federal funds rate (e.g., 5.25% to 5.50%). The Fed uses its tools to keep the effective federal funds rate within this range.

Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR):
The volume-weighted median of overnight federal funds transactions reported by major brokers. Published daily by the New York Fed.

Transmission Mechanism:
Federal funds rate → other short-term rates (SOFR, T-bills) → longer-term rates (mortgages, corporate bonds) → asset prices (stocks, real estate) → exchange rate → consumption, investment, net exports → aggregate demand → inflation and employment.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine a large bank, Bank A, has \$100 million more in reserves than it needs for its daily operations. Another bank, Bank B, is short \$100 million because a large corporate customer wired money out. Bank B could borrow from Bank A overnight at the federal funds rate. If the rate is 5.25%, Bank B pays Bank A interest on the \$100 million loan. The next day, Bank B repays the loan, and both banks' reserves return to normal. This overnight market for reserves is the federal funds market.

The Fed cannot directly force banks to lend at a specific rate. Instead, it influences the rate through the supply of reserves and the interest it pays on reserves. When the Fed wants to lower the federal funds rate, it increases the supply of reserves (by buying bonds) or lowers IORB. Banks have more reserves than they need, so they are willing to lend at lower rates. When the Fed wants to raise the federal funds rate, it decreases the supply of reserves (by selling bonds) or raises IORB, making banks less willing to lend cheaply.

The federal funds rate is the most important interest rate in the economy because it anchors all other interest rates. When the Fed raises the federal funds rate, banks raise their prime rate (the rate they charge their best customers), mortgage rates rise, credit card rates rise, and corporate borrowing costs increase. This slows spending and cools the economy. When the Fed lowers the federal funds rate, borrowing becomes cheaper, stimulating spending and boosting the economy.

image.alt: Graph: Supply and demand for reserves; vertical supply curve (determined by Fed) and downward-sloping demand curve; intersection determines federal funds rate; IORB shown as floor.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.1: The federal funds rate is determined by the supply and demand for reserves in the overnight interbank lending market.

sections.introduction:
The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks) lend reserve balances to each other overnight on an uncollateralized basis. It is the most closely watched interest rate in the world because it is the primary policy target of the Federal Reserve. By setting a target for the federal funds rate and using its monetary policy tools to keep the actual rate within that target range, the Fed influences all other interest rates—from mortgage rates to corporate bond yields—and ultimately economic activity, employment, and inflation.

Understanding the federal funds rate is essential for analyzing how the Fed implements monetary policy, how changes in policy transmit to financial markets, and why the federal funds rate is the benchmark for short-term interest rates globally. The Fed does not directly control the federal funds rate; rather, it influences it through the supply of reserves and the interest rate it pays on reserves (IORB). The actual federal funds rate is determined by the supply and demand for reserves in the overnight interbank lending market.

In this section, we will examine the mechanics of the federal funds market, how the Fed sets and implements its target, the relationship between the federal funds rate and other interest rates (the transmission mechanism), the role of the federal funds rate in monetary policy rules (Taylor rule), and the history of federal funds targeting including the zero lower bound period.

introduction_image.alt: Time series graph: Effective federal funds rate (1954-2024); spikes in 1980s (Volcker); steady decline after 2000; near zero 2008-2015; rising 2022-2024.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 8.2.2: Historical effective federal funds rate: from the Volcker era (20% rates) to the zero lower bound (2008-2015) to the 2022-2024 tightening cycle.

key_concept.definition:
Federal Funds Rate: The overnight interest rate at which depository institutions lend reserve balances to each other on an uncollateralized basis. It is the Federal Reserve's primary monetary policy target.

Key Participants:
• Commercial banks (large and small)
• Savings institutions (thrifts, credit unions)
• Government-sponsored enterprises (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, FHLB)
• Primary dealers (through repurchase agreements, though those are collateralized)

Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR):
The volume-weighted median of overnight federal funds transactions reported daily by major brokers. Published by the New York Fed at 9:00 AM ET.

FOMC Target Range:
The FOMC announces a target range (e.g., 5.25% to 5.50%) for the federal funds rate. The Fed uses its tools to keep the EFFR within this range.

Implementation Tools (Ample Reserves Framework):
• IORB (Interest on Reserve Balances): Acts as a floor; banks will not lend reserves below IORB.
• ON RRP (Overnight Reverse Repo Facility): Acts as a floor for non-banks; money market funds lend to Fed at ON RRP rate.
• Discount window: Acts as a ceiling (rarely used).

Transmission Mechanism:
Federal funds rate → SOFR, T-bills, prime rate → mortgage rates, corporate bond yields, auto loans → consumption, investment → aggregate demand → inflation, employment.

Taylor Rule:
A simple policy rule linking the federal funds rate to inflation and output: $i = r^* + \pi + 0.5(\pi - \pi^*) + 0.5(Y - Y^*)$. The Fed does not mechanically follow the Taylor rule but uses it as a guideline.
key_concept.context: The federal funds market emerged in the 1920s as banks began trading reserves. The Fed began targeting the federal funds rate in the 1970s as a more precise alternative to targeting money supply. From 1979-1982, the Fed experimented with money supply targeting (the monetarist experiment), but the federal funds rate became extremely volatile. After 1982, the Fed returned to federal funds rate targeting. The 2008 crisis led to the zero lower bound and the introduction of IORB. Since 2008, the Fed has operated an 'ample reserves' framework where the federal funds rate is determined by IORB and ON RRP floors rather than active reserve management. The federal funds rate remains the primary policy signal, though its importance has diminished slightly as the Fed now also uses forward guidance and balance sheet policy as communication tools.

image.alt: Graph: Federal funds target range (shaded band) and effective federal funds rate (line) 2020-2024; EFFR stays within range; tight spread shows successful implementation.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.3: The effective federal funds rate (EFFR) typically stays within the FOMC's target range, demonstrating successful policy implementation.

classifications.type: Federal Funds Rate Determination: Scarce vs. Ample Reserves
classifications.value: The framework for federal funds rate determination changed fundamentally after 2008
characteristics: Pre-2008 (scarce reserves): Reserves scarce; federal funds rate determined by supply/demand; Fed used OMOs to shift supply
Post-2008 (ample reserves): Reserves abundant; federal funds rate determined by IORB floor; Fed uses IORB as primary tool
IORB creates a floor: Banks will not lend reserves below IORB (can earn IORB risk-free)
ON RRP creates a second floor for non-banks
Discount window provides ceiling (rarely binding)
Federal funds rate trades between ON RRP rate and IORB
classifications.behavior:
In the scarce reserves framework (pre-2008), banks held only enough reserves to meet requirements. The federal funds rate was determined by the intersection of a vertical supply curve (set by the Fed via OMOs) and a downward-sloping demand curve (banks' need for reserves). The Fed conducted daily open market operations to keep the supply at the level that produced the target rate. This required precise forecasting of reserve demand.

In the ample reserves framework (post-2008), banks hold trillions in excess reserves (earning IORB). The demand for reserves becomes flat at the IORB rate because banks are indifferent between holding reserves (earning IORB) and lending them. The federal funds rate cannot fall below IORB (banks would rather earn IORB). Non-banks cannot earn IORB, so the Fed offers the ON RRP facility as a floor for them. The federal funds rate (only banks) trades between ON RRP and IORB.

This framework is much simpler: the Fed sets IORB and ON RRP, and the market determines the federal funds rate within that band. The Fed does not need to forecast reserve demand or conduct daily OMOs. This is why the Fed has kept its balance sheet large—to maintain ample reserves and keep the floor system working.

image.alt: Two graphs side by side: Left (scarce reserves) - vertical supply, downward-sloping demand, OMO shifts supply. Right (ample reserves) - horizontal demand at IORB, supply far to the right.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.4: Scarce reserves (pre-2008) vs. ample reserves (post-2008) frameworks for federal funds rate determination.

examples.explanation: In September 2007, the Fed wanted to lower the federal funds rate from 5.25% to 4.75%. It conducted open market purchases of Treasury bonds, injecting reserves. The increased supply of reserves pushed the federal funds rate down to the new target. The Fed then conducted daily fine-tuning OMOs to keep the rate at 4.75%.

In March 2022, the Fed wanted to raise the federal funds rate from 0.25% to 0.50%. It raised IORB from 0.40% to 0.65% and ON RRP from 0.30% to 0.55%. The federal funds rate immediately rose to the new range (trading between ON RRP and IORB). No active OMOs were needed.

classifications.type: FOMC Target Communication and the Dot Plot
classifications.value: The FOMC communicates its federal funds rate target through statements, press conferences, and the Summary of Economic Projections (SEP)
characteristics: Policy statement: Released at 2:00 PM ET after each FOMC meeting, announces target range
Press conference: Chair explains decision and provides forward guidance (quarterly meetings)
Summary of Economic Projections (SEP): Released quarterly, includes 'dot plot' of individual members' rate forecasts
Dot plot shows each FOMC member's projection of the appropriate federal funds rate at year-end for the next several years
Minutes: Detailed record released 3 weeks after meeting
Forward guidance: Language in statement about future policy (e.g., 'patient,' 'data-dependent')
classifications.behavior:
The FOMC's communication strategy has evolved significantly. Before 1994, the Fed did not announce rate changes; markets had to infer policy from open market operations. In 1994, the Fed began announcing target changes immediately. In 2000, the Fed began issuing a policy statement after every meeting. In 2011, then-Chair Bernanke began holding regular press conferences. In 2012, the Fed began publishing the SEP (including the dot plot).

The dot plot is a scatter plot showing each FOMC member's projection of the federal funds rate at the end of the current year and the next several years, as well as the longer-run normal rate. The median dot is often interpreted as the market's expected policy path. However, individual dots are not commitments; they are conditional forecasts. Markets scrutinize the dot plot for clues about future policy.

Forward guidance is the Fed's communication about the likely future path of policy. It can be calendar-based ('rates will stay low until at least 2024') or state-contingent ('rates will stay low until inflation averages 2%'). Forward guidance is a powerful tool, especially at the zero lower bound, because it influences long-term interest rates even when the current federal funds rate is stuck at zero.

image.alt: Scatter plot: Each dot is an FOMC member's projection of federal funds rate at year-end 2024, 2025, 2026, and longer-run; median dots connected by line.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.5: The FOMC's dot plot shows individual members' rate projections; the median is often interpreted as the market's expected policy path.

examples.explanation: The December 2023 dot plot showed a median projection of 4.6% for end-2024 (implying three 25bp rate cuts), 3.6% for end-2025, and 2.5% for the longer-run neutral rate. Markets reacted with a rally, pricing in more cuts than the Fed had previously signaled.

In August 2020, the Fed adopted Average Inflation Targeting (AIT), stating that it would keep rates near zero until inflation averaged 2% over time. This forward guidance lowered long-term rates even though the federal funds rate was already at zero, providing additional stimulus.

classifications.type: The Federal Funds Rate and Monetary Policy Transmission
classifications.value: Changes in the federal funds rate affect the broader economy through multiple channels
characteristics: Interest rate channel: Fed funds → other short-term rates → long-term rates → borrowing costs → spending
Credit channel: Fed funds → bank lending standards → credit availability → investment
Asset price channel: Fed funds → stock prices, real estate prices → wealth effect → consumption
Exchange rate channel: Fed funds → interest rate differential → exchange rate → net exports
Expectations channel: Fed funds → market expectations of future policy → long-term rates (forward guidance)
Transmission lags: Typically 12-24 months for full effect on inflation
classifications.behavior:
The federal funds rate is the starting point of the monetary policy transmission mechanism. When the Fed raises the federal funds rate, the effects ripple through the economy:

Interest rate channel: Banks raise their prime rate (typically fed funds + 3%). Mortgage rates, auto loan rates, credit card rates, and corporate bond yields all rise. Higher borrowing costs reduce consumption of durable goods (cars, appliances) and business investment (machinery, buildings).

Credit channel: Higher rates may cause banks to tighten lending standards (especially for small businesses), further reducing credit availability.

Asset price channel: Higher rates reduce the present value of future earnings, lowering stock prices (the 'Fed model'). Lower stock prices reduce household wealth, decreasing consumption (wealth effect).

Exchange rate channel: Higher US interest rates attract foreign capital, causing the dollar to appreciate. A stronger dollar makes US exports more expensive abroad and imports cheaper, reducing net exports.

Expectations channel: Market participants anticipate future rate changes. The Fed's forward guidance can influence long-term rates even before the Fed acts.

The full effect of a federal funds rate change on inflation takes 12-24 months. This is why the Fed must be forward-looking, adjusting policy based on forecasts rather than current inflation.

image.alt: Flowchart: Fed funds rate → short-term rates → long-term rates, asset prices, exchange rate, credit conditions → consumption, investment, net exports → aggregate demand → inflation and employment.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.6: The monetary policy transmission mechanism: changes in the federal funds rate affect the economy through multiple channels with long and variable lags.

examples.explanation: The Fed raised the federal funds rate from 0.25% to 5.33% in 11 meetings (2022-2023). Mortgage rates rose from 3% to 7%, housing sales fell 40%. Auto loan rates rose from 4% to 9%, auto sales fell. Corporate bond yields rose, stock prices fell in 2022 (S&P 500 -20%). The dollar appreciated 15%, reducing net exports. By 2024, inflation fell from 9% to 3%.

In July-October 2019, the Fed cut the federal funds rate from 2.50% to 1.75% (three 25bp cuts). Mortgage rates fell from 4.5% to 3.7%, boosting housing. Stock prices rose 10%. The dollar depreciated modestly. The easing helped sustain the expansion through 2019-2020 until the pandemic.

classifications.type: The Taylor Rule and Federal Funds Rate Policy
classifications.value: The Taylor Rule is a simple formula linking the federal funds rate to inflation and output; it serves as a benchmark for policy
characteristics: Original Taylor rule (1993): $i = r^* + \pi + 0.5(\pi - \pi^*) + 0.5(Y - Y^*)$
$r^*$ = neutral real rate (often assumed 2%)
$\pi$ = actual inflation (GDP deflator or PCE)
$\pi^*$ = inflation target (2%)
$(Y - Y^*)$ = output gap (percent deviation from potential)
Coefficients (0.5) reflect Fed's preference for stabilizing both inflation and output
Modified Taylor rules include different coefficients, lagged rates, or different inflation measures
classifications.behavior:
The Taylor rule (developed by Stanford economist John Taylor) provides a guideline for what the federal funds rate should be given current economic conditions. If inflation is above target, the rule recommends raising rates; if output is below potential, the rule recommends lowering rates. The coefficients (0.5 each) imply that the Fed should raise the federal funds rate by 1.5 percentage points for every 1 percentage point increase in inflation (0.5 from the direct term plus 0.5 from the output gap if inflation is caused by overheating).

The Fed does not mechanically follow the Taylor rule. In the 2000s, the actual federal funds rate was below the Taylor rule recommendation (contributing to the housing bubble). In the 2010s, the actual rate was also below the rule (due to the zero lower bound). In the 2020s, the actual rate has roughly followed the rule. The Taylor rule remains a useful benchmark for evaluating whether policy is too tight or too loose.

Critics argue that the Taylor rule (1) assumes we know the neutral rate $r^*$ (which is unobservable), (2) ignores financial stability concerns, (3) is too simplistic for complex economic environments. Despite these criticisms, the Taylor rule is widely used by investors and policymakers as a reference point.

image.alt: Graph: Actual federal funds rate vs. Taylor rule prescription (1990-2024); periods of divergence (2000s too low, 2010s too high due to ZLB) highlighted.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.7: The Taylor rule provides a benchmark for the federal funds rate; deviations indicate policy that is looser or tighter than historical norms.

examples.explanation: In 2022, inflation was 7%, inflation target 2%, output gap near zero, neutral rate 2%. Taylor rule: i = 2% + 7% + 0.5(7%-2%) + 0.5(0) = 2% + 7% + 2.5% = 11.5%. The actual federal funds rate was 0.25-0.50%—far below the rule, indicating extremely loose policy. The Fed began raising rates aggressively in March 2022, catching up to the Taylor rule.

In the 2000s, the actual federal funds rate was well below the Taylor rule recommendation (rates were 'too low for too long'). Critics argue this contributed to the housing bubble and 2008 crisis. The Fed responded that inflation was low, so low rates were appropriate; the Taylor rule failed to account for financial stability risks.

principles.principle: The Federal Funds Rate Is the Most Important Interest Rate in the Economy Because It Anchors All Other Interest Rates
principles.statement: The federal funds rate is the benchmark for short-term risk-free rates in the United States. Through arbitrage and expectations, changes in the federal funds rate transmit to every other interest rate: Treasury bills, SOFR, prime rate, LIBOR (now discontinued), commercial paper, corporate bonds, mortgages, auto loans, and credit cards. The transmission is not one-for-one (e.g., mortgage rates also depend on term premiums and MBS spreads), but the direction and rough magnitude are clear. When the Fed raises the federal funds rate by 1 percentage point, 2-year Treasury yields typically rise by 0.8-1.0 percentage points, 10-year yields by 0.3-0.6 percentage points, and mortgage rates by 0.5-0.8 percentage points. This transmission is why the federal funds rate is the primary tool for influencing economic activity and inflation.
principles.application: Investors and policymakers should monitor the spread between the federal funds rate and other rates to gauge financial conditions. A widening spread (e.g., mortgage rates rising faster than fed funds) indicates tighter financial conditions beyond what the Fed has directly implemented. A narrowing spread indicates that markets are pricing in future rate cuts or that risk premiums are compressing. The term spread (10-year minus 2-year) is a predictor of recessions; an inverted curve (long rates below short rates) has preceded every US recession since 1970.
principles.example: In 2022, the Fed raised the federal funds rate from 0.25% to 4.50%. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate rose from 3% to 7%—a 4 percentage point increase, roughly in line with the fed funds increase plus a widening MBS spread. A household borrowing \$300,000 saw their monthly payment rise from \$1,265 to \$1,996 (58% increase). This dramatic increase in borrowing costs cooled the housing market and contributed to disinflation.

image.alt: Graph: Fed funds rate, 2-year Treasury, 10-year Treasury, mortgage rates (2020-2024); all rise together but with different magnitudes; spreads shown.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.8: Changes in the federal funds rate transmit to other interest rates, though not one-for-one due to term premiums and risk spreads.

principles.principle: The Fed Cannot Set the Federal Funds Rate Directly; It Influences It Through IORB and the Supply of Reserves
principles.statement: The federal funds rate is a market-determined interest rate. The Fed does not dictate that banks must lend at a specific rate. Instead, the Fed uses its tools to create conditions such that the market rate falls within the target range. In the ample reserves framework, the primary tool is IORB (interest on reserves). Banks will not lend reserves below IORB because they can earn IORB risk-free by holding reserves. Thus, IORB acts as a floor. The ON RRP facility provides a floor for non-banks. The discount window acts as a ceiling (though rarely binding). The federal funds rate then trades between these floors. This framework is reliable and does not require the Fed to forecast reserve demand or conduct daily OMOs. The Fed's ability to control the federal funds rate is what gives it power over the economy.
principles.application: When analyzing Fed policy, focus on IORB and the ON RRP rate, not just the fed funds target range. The Fed communicates its target range, but the actual implementation is through IORB (set near the top of the range) and ON RRP (set near the bottom). The spread between IORB and the effective federal funds rate indicates how well the floor system is working. A very tight spread (e.g., 1-2 basis points) indicates the floor is binding. A wider spread indicates that non-banks are playing a larger role in funding markets.
principles.example: In 2023, the Fed's target range was 5.25-5.50%. IORB was set at 5.40%, ON RRP at 5.30%. The effective federal funds rate traded at 5.33%—almost exactly halfway between the two floors. The spread between IORB and EFFR was 7 basis points, indicating a well-functioning floor system. The Fed did not need to conduct any open market operations to achieve this; the floors did the work.

image.alt: Graph: IORB (5.40%), ON RRP (5.30%), EFFR (5.33%), target range (5.25-5.50%); EFFR between floors; discount window (5.75%) as ceiling.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.9: The Fed controls the federal funds rate through IORB (floor for banks) and ON RRP (floor for non-banks); the market determines the exact rate within the band.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Taylor Rule Prescription for the Federal Funds Rate

image.caption: Figure 8.2.10: Worked example: applying the Taylor rule to compute the recommended federal funds rate.

worked_examples.problem:
Use the Taylor rule to calculate the recommended federal funds rate given the following economic conditions:

• Actual inflation (PCE) = 3.0%
• Inflation target = 2.0%
• Output gap = +1.0% (economy 1% above potential)
• Neutral real rate (r*) = 2.0% (estimate of the natural rate)

a) Calculate the Taylor rule prescription using the original coefficients (0.5 for both gaps).
b) If the output gap were -2% (recession), recalculate.
c) If inflation were 1.5% (below target) and output gap -1%, recalculate.
d) The Taylor rule assumes r* = 2%. If r* has fallen to 0.5% (secular stagnation), recalculate part (a).
e) Why might the Fed deviate from the Taylor rule prescription?

step_by_step.explanation:
Inflation still 3%, above target. Output gap = -2% (recession).

$i = 2.0\% + 3.0\% + 0.5(1.0\%) + 0.5(-2.0\%)$
$i = 2.0\% + 3.0\% + 0.5\% - 1.0\% = 4.5\%$

The rule recommends a lower rate (4.5% vs. 6.0%) because the recession (negative output gap) calls for easing, partially offsetting the need to fight inflation.

step_by_step.step: Calculate with below-target inflation and negative output gap
step_by_step.explanation:
Inflation = 1.5% (below 2% target), output gap = -1%.

$i = 2.0\% + 1.5\% + 0.5(1.5\% - 2.0\%) + 0.5(-1.0\%)$
$i = 2.0\% + 1.5\% + 0.5(-0.5\%) + 0.5(-1.0\%)$
$i = 2.0\% + 1.5\% - 0.25\% - 0.5\% = 2.75\%$

The rule recommends a relatively low rate (2.75%) because both inflation is below target and output is below potential. This would be an easing scenario.

If the neutral real rate has fallen to 0.5% (due to secular stagnation, aging population, low productivity), then:

$i = 0.5\% + 3.0\% + 0.5(1.0\%) + 0.5(1.0\%)$
$i = 0.5\% + 3.0\% + 0.5\% + 0.5\% = 4.5\%$

With a lower r*, the recommended federal funds rate is 1.5 percentage points lower (4.5% vs. 6.0%). This illustrates why the Fed cut rates in 2019 even though inflation was at target—the natural rate had fallen, so the neutral policy rate was lower.

step_by_step.step: Explain why Fed might deviate from Taylor rule
step_by_step.explanation:
The Fed may deviate from the Taylor rule for several reasons:
1. Uncertainty about r*: The natural rate is unobservable; different estimates give different prescriptions.
2. Financial stability concerns: The Taylor rule ignores asset bubbles and credit growth. The Fed may raise rates to cool a housing bubble even if inflation is low (Greenspan's 'maestro' era, or lack thereof).
3. Zero lower bound: The Taylor rule may prescribe negative rates (e.g., -2%), but the Fed cannot go below zero. It must use QE and forward guidance instead.
4. Lags and uncertainty: The output gap and inflation are measured with error and subject to revision. The Fed may be more cautious than the rule.
5. Multiple mandates: The Fed has a dual mandate (maximum employment and price stability). The Taylor rule weights them equally, but the Fed may adjust weights.
6. Forward guidance: The Fed may promise to keep rates lower for longer than the rule suggests to anchor expectations.

Despite these caveats, the Taylor rule is a useful benchmark. When the actual fed funds rate deviates significantly from the rule, it signals that policy is either unusually loose or unusually tight, which often predicts future policy changes.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 6.0%. b) 4.5%. c) 2.75%. d) 4.5%. e) Uncertainty about r*, financial stability concerns, ZLB, measurement errors, dual mandate, forward guidance.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Taylor rule to compute recommended federal funds rate under different economic conditions and understanding why the Fed might deviate.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Federal Funds Rate Transmission to Mortgage Rates

image.alt: Table: Fed funds rate changes (2022-2023) and corresponding mortgage rate changes; spread analysis; monthly payment calculation.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.11: Worked example: tracing the transmission from federal funds rate changes to mortgage rates and borrower payments.

worked_examples.problem:
A homebuyer is considering a \$300,000 30-year fixed-rate mortgage. The federal funds rate is currently 5.25%. The mortgage rate is typically the 10-year Treasury yield plus a spread (about 2.5% for mortgage-backed securities). The 10-year Treasury yield is approximately the average of expected future federal funds rates over 10 years plus a term premium (currently 0.5%).

a) If markets expect the federal funds rate to average 3.5% over the next 10 years, what is the 10-year Treasury yield (assume term premium = 0.5%)?
b) What is the mortgage rate (add 2.5% spread)?
c) What is the monthly payment on a \$300,000 mortgage at this rate?
d) If the Fed raises the federal funds rate by 1% and markets expect the average future rate to rise to 4.0%, recalculate the mortgage rate and monthly payment.
e) Explain why mortgage rates rose faster than the federal funds rate in 2022-2023.

step_by_step.explanation:
10-year Treasury yield = average expected future fed funds rate + term premium
= 3.5% + 0.5% = 4.0%

Mortgage rate = 10-year Treasury yield + MBS spread
= 4.0% + 2.5% = 6.5%

Monthly payment formula: $P = \frac{r \times Principal}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$ where $r$ is monthly rate, $n$ is number of months.

Monthly rate = 6.5% / 12 = 0.54167% = 0.0054167
n = 30 × 12 = 360 months

$P = \frac{0.0054167 \times 300,000}{1 - (1.0054167)^{-360}}$
$P = \frac{1,625}{1 - 0.144} = \frac{1,625}{0.856} = \$1,898$ per month

Expected average future fed funds rate rises from 3.5% to 4.0%.

New 10-year Treasury yield = 4.0% + 0.5% = 4.5%
New mortgage rate = 4.5% + 2.5% = 7.0%

Monthly rate = 7.0% / 12 = 0.58333% = 0.0058333

$P = \frac{0.0058333 \times 300,000}{1 - (1.0058333)^{-360}} = \frac{1,750}{1 - 0.124} = \frac{1,750}{0.876} = \$1,998$ per month

Monthly payment increased by \$100 (5.3%) from a 0.5 percentage point increase in the 10-year yield (and 0.5% mortgage rate increase).

step_by_step.step: Explain why mortgage rates rose faster than fed funds in 2022-2023
step_by_step.explanation:
From 2022-2023, the Fed raised the federal funds rate from 0.25% to 5.33% (a 5.08 percentage point increase). The 30-year mortgage rate rose from 3% to 7% (a 4 percentage point increase). Mortgage rates rose roughly in line with fed funds but with some differences:

1. Term premiums increased: As the Fed tightened, uncertainty about future rates increased, raising term premiums on long-term bonds.
2. MBS spreads widened: Mortgage-backed securities became less liquid, and prepayment risk increased, widening the spread over Treasuries.
3. Expectations channel: Markets expected the Fed to keep rates high for longer, raising the average expected future fed funds rate more than the current rate.
4. Quantitative tightening (QT): The Fed stopped buying MBS and allowed its portfolio to run off, reducing demand and raising MBS yields.

In 2022, mortgage rates actually rose faster than fed funds (mortgage rates up 3% while fed funds up 2% in the first half). This was due to the Fed's balance sheet runoff (QT) and increased term premiums. By 2023, the relationship stabilized, with mortgage rates roughly 1.5-2.0% above the 10-year Treasury yield.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 4.0%. b) 6.5%. c) \$1,898/month. d) 10-year = 4.5%, mortgage = 7.0%, payment = \$1,998/month. e) Rising term premiums, widening MBS spreads, QT, and higher expected future rates caused mortgage rates to rise faster than the federal funds rate in 2022-2023.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the transmission mechanism from federal funds rate to mortgage rates and calculating the impact on borrower payments.

process_flow.process: FOMC sets target range for federal funds rate
process_flow.description: The FOMC meets 8 times per year and announces a target range (e.g., 5.25-5.50%) based on its economic projections.
process_flow.mechanism: FOMC reviews staff forecasts (Tealbook), Beige Book, and member projections. The Chair builds consensus, and the committee votes. The target is announced at 2:00 PM ET.

image.alt: Diagram: FOMC meeting → economic data review → consensus building → vote → target range announcement.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.12: Step 1 - The FOMC sets the target range for the federal funds rate based on economic conditions.

process_flow.process: Fed sets IORB and ON RRP rates relative to target
process_flow.description: The Fed sets IORB near the top of the target range and ON RRP near the bottom to create floors for banks and non-banks.
process_flow.mechanism: IORB is typically set at the top of the target range (e.g., 5.40% for a 5.25-5.50% range). ON RRP is set 5-15bp below IORB (e.g., 5.30%).

image.alt: Diagram: Target range (5.25-5.50%) → IORB set at 5.40% → ON RRP set at 5.30% → floors created.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.13: Step 2 - The Fed sets IORB and ON RRP to create a floor system for the federal funds rate.

process_flow.process: Banks and non-banks transact in federal funds and repo markets
process_flow.description: Banks with excess reserves lend to banks with deficits at rates between ON RRP and IORB. Non-banks use the ON RRP facility.
process_flow.mechanism: The effective federal funds rate (EFFR) is determined by supply and demand within the floor system. The Fed publishes EFFR daily.

image.alt: Diagram: Banks lend reserves at rates between ON RRP and IORB → EFFR determined → published by NY Fed.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.14: Step 3 - The market determines the effective federal funds rate within the floor system.

process_flow.process: Changes in EFFR transmit to other interest rates
process_flow.description: The federal funds rate influences short-term rates (SOFR, T-bills), which influence long-term rates (mortgages, corporate bonds).
process_flow.mechanism: Arbitrage and expectations link the federal funds rate to other rates. The transmission is not instantaneous; lags vary.

image.alt: Diagram: EFFR → SOFR/T-bills → 2-year Treasury → 10-year Treasury → mortgage rates, corporate bonds → borrowing costs.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.15: Step 4 - Changes in the federal funds rate transmit through the yield curve to borrowing costs for households and firms.

process_flow.process: Borrowing costs affect spending, output, and inflation
process_flow.description: Higher borrowing costs reduce consumption and investment, slowing aggregate demand and eventually reducing inflation.
process_flow.mechanism: Transmission lags: 6-12 months for output, 12-24 months for inflation. The Fed monitors these effects to adjust policy.

image.alt: Diagram: Borrowing costs → consumption, investment → aggregate demand → output gap → inflation → FOMC reviews data (feedback loop).
image.caption: Figure 8.2.16: Step 5 - Changes in borrowing costs affect the real economy with lags, feeding back into the FOMC's policy decisions.

image.alt: Master framework: FOMC sets target → IORB/ON RRP floors → federal funds market → EFFR → transmission to other rates → borrowing costs → spending → output/inflation → feedback to FOMC.
image.caption: Figure 8.2.17: Complete framework for the federal funds rate: from policy setting to economic outcomes and feedback.

summary: The federal funds rate is the overnight interest rate at which banks lend reserve balances to each other and the primary policy target of the Federal Reserve. The FOMC sets a target range (e.g., 5.25-5.50%), and the Fed implements policy using IORB (interest on reserves) as a floor for banks and ON RRP as a floor for non-banks, with the discount window as a backstop ceiling. In the ample reserves framework (post-2008), the effective federal funds rate trades between these floors, and the Fed does not need to conduct daily open market operations. Changes in the federal funds rate transmit through the economy via the interest rate channel (borrowing costs), credit channel (lending standards), asset price channel (wealth effect), exchange rate channel (net exports), and expectations channel (forward guidance). The Taylor rule provides a benchmark for the appropriate federal funds rate based on inflation and the output gap. The Fed's communication strategy includes policy statements, press conferences, the Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), and the dot plot of individual members' rate projections. Understanding the federal funds rate is essential for analyzing monetary policy, forecasting interest rates, and evaluating the stance of policy.
